moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the English Opening, one of the most flexible first moves in chess. By playing c4, we control the d5 square and prepare to build a setup around the long diagonal and queenside expansion. This move lets us avoid the theoretical battles of 1.e4 while maintaining plenty of winning chances—it's a favorite of positional players who enjoy maneuvering games over sharp tactical play.

Black plays the provocative Myers Defense, immediately launching a flank attack on the kingside. This move is quite unusual and aggressive: Black weakens the kingside (especially f5 and h5), but gains space and challenges us to react. While 1...g5 is rare at high levels because it violates several opening principles, it can catch unprepared opponents off-guard. We should calmly continue developing and look to exploit the weaknesses Black has created on the kingside.
